---
title: "Add raster in QGIS"
description: |
  In this blog post, we'll explore add a raster layer with the example of bathymetry data in QGIS. 
author:
  - name: Miriam Lerma
    url: {}
date: 2026-06-25
image: raster_front.png
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-location: left
categories:
  - qgis
  - Y2026
listing: false
---

# Intro

In this blog post, we'll explore add a raster layer with the example of bathymetry data in QGIS. In addition, how to export the map including legelds in the preferred format. 

```{r, echo=FALSE}
library(here)
import_directory<-paste0(here(),"/blog/2026-06-25-qgis-raster")
```

## Download

To download the data use 
GEBCO: General Bathymetry Chart of the Oceans provides information from bathymetry in the ocean.

To download visit webpage


Go to:
- Download data for user-defined areas
- Click on application

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width = "90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(import_directory,"/gebco.png"))
```

- Add your coordinates -here I use 9 to 16 and 56 to 53.
- I also selected GeoTIFF

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width = "90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(import_directory,"/grid.png"))
```

- Add to basket and download

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width = "90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(import_directory,"/download.png"))
```

## QGIS

Add layer

To add raster layer go to: Layer > Add Layer > Add Raster Layer

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width = "90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(import_directory,"/add_raster.png"))
```

Select the file from Bathymetry.
For my case the file will start with "gebco"
When loading it would appear in grey colors

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width = "90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(import_directory,"/in_grey.png"))
```

To change to blues go to:

Properties> Symbology
Select
Render type: Singleband pseudocolor
Here I changed to Min -200 and Max 0 as I am interested in water depth
Interpolation: Linear
Color ramp: Blues (if necesary select invert color ramp after clicking o the color)
Mode: Continuos 
Click in Classify, you can adapt the classess and the categories.

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width = "90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(import_directory,"/symbology.png"))
```

## Layout

For this layout, I go to Project > Layout section.

Once the layout is open I add the legend in Add Item > Add Legend
De select all the other layers, and only select this layer.
Remove the auto update, 
Use the arrows to reorganize the order of the legends,
Double click to change the legend to ">0" if is the case.

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width = "30%"}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(import_directory,"/change_legend.png"))
```

I additionally added some text of areas that could be of interest.
Names were extracted from: https://os.copernicus.org/articles/15/905/2019/

# Export

Here is the final map, with the water depth, the legend of the water depth, and the names from specific basins. 

I got inspiration from this publication [link here](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/305690503_Comparing_Distribution_of_Harbour_Porpoises_Phocoena_phocoena_Derived_from_Satellite_Telemetry_and_Passive_Acoustic_Monitoring/figures?lo=1) and names from [here](https://os.copernicus.org/articles/15/905/2019/)

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width = "90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(import_directory,"/BalticSea_06Bathy.jpeg"))
```

# Next post

How to add side by side maps to describe the general larger area, including maritime areas and borders.
